sections.title: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility

bullet_points: 1A. Overview of SC Alcohol Laws + Licensing & Permits.
1B. Underage Sales Laws, Penalties, and Server Responsibility.
1C. Serving Intoxicated Persons + DUI Laws & Consequences.
1D. Concealed Weapons + Legal Authority + Enforcement Overview.
1E. Criminal, Civil, Administrative Liability + Real Consequences.
text.body: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: South Carolina issues different permits depending on what a business sells and when. An On-Premises Beer & Wine Permit (PBW) lets a business sell beer and wine for on-site drinking and to-go, up to six days a week, twenty-four hours a day. Sunday sales are only allowed if the county has approved a Local Option. A 7-Day On-Premises Beer & Wine Permit (PO7) allows beer and wine sales seven days a week, except Sundays from 2 a.m. to 10 a.m., and is only issued in counties that approved Sunday sales by referendum. Knowing your establishment's permit tells you exactly what you are allowed to serve and when. Relevant statutes: SC Code Sections 61-4-120, 61-4-510, and 61-4-630.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Liquor by the drink requires its own permit. A Business (Restaurant & Hotel) Liquor by the Drink License (PLB) covers businesses focused on meals or lodging and allows liquor Monday through Saturday, 10 a.m. to 2 a.m. A Nonprofit (Private Club) Liquor by the Drink License (PLC) covers valid nonprofit organizations seven days a week, 10 a.m. to 2 a.m. A Local Option Permit (LOP) adds Sunday liquor service for businesses that already hold a PLB, but only in counties that approved Sunday liquor sales. Serving outside your permit's hours or type is an illegal sale. Relevant statutes: SC Code Sections 61-6-1600 and 61-6-1610.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Every permit holder must display their alcohol licenses in a clearly visible place on the premises. This lets regulators, law enforcement, and the public confirm the business is authorized to sell alcohol. As a server, you should know where your establishment's permits are posted. Relevant statute: SC Code of Regulations 7-200.3.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Operating a retail or wholesale alcohol business without a permit is a misdemeanor punishable by a fine of $10 to $100 or imprisonment of 10 to 30 days. Each day the business operates without a permit is a separate offense, and all beer, wine, and liquor on the premises becomes contraband that the State Law Enforcement Division (SLED) must seize. This is why permits and their renewal are taken seriously. Relevant statutes: SC Code Sections 61-4-150, 61-4-560, 61-6-2600, and 61-6-2610.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina law states a person may not enter a business that sells alcohol for on-premises consumption while carrying a firearm and then consume alcohol. Violating this is a misdemeanor punishable by a fine of up to $2,000 or up to two years in prison, or both, and a Concealed Weapons Permit is revoked for five years. A business may also post a 'NO CONCEALABLE WEAPONS ALLOWED' sign; a person who refuses to comply can be fined up to $200 or jailed up to 30 days. Relevant statutes: SC Code Sections 16-23-465 and 23-31-220.

interactive.question: Your restaurant holds a PLB (Business Liquor by the Drink) license but not a Local Option Permit. A guest asks for a cocktail at 1 p.m. on Sunday. What is correct?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Serve it; liquor by the drink is always allowed
B. Do not serve liquor by the drink on Sunday without a Local Option Permit
C. Serve only if they also order food
D. Serve a double instead

feedback.correct: A PLB allows liquor Monday-Saturday. Sunday liquor by the drink requires a Local Option Permit (LOP), issued only where the county approved Sunday liquor sales.

paragraphs: South Carolina alcohol laws are enforced at the state level. These laws control who can sell alcohol, how it is served, and when service is allowed. As a server, you must follow both state law and your workplace policies. Failing to follow the law can lead to penalties for you and your employer. Your role directly impacts legal compliance and public safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol sales in South Carolina require proper licensing. Businesses must hold a valid permit before selling beer, wine, or liquor. Different permits apply depending on the type of alcohol and business model. For example, restaurants, bars, and retail stores may have different requirements. Main on-premises permit types include PBW, PO7, PLB, PLC, and LOP. Always work under a valid license. Serving alcohol without proper authorization is illegal. References: SC Code § 61-4-120; § 61-4-510; § 61-4-630; § 61-6-1600; § 61-6-1610; § 61-4-150; § 61-4-560; § 61-6-2600; § 61-6-2610.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: South Carolina alcohol laws are enforced by agencies such as SLED (South Carolina Law Enforcement Division) and local authorities, while ABL (Alcohol Beverage Licensing) manages licenses and permits. These laws control who can sell alcohol, when to serve, and where alcohol is allowed. Businesses must follow license rules and maintain legal compliance. After completing training and program requirements, participants may access certificates and state resources through the MYDORWAY portal when applicable. As a server, you are responsible for understanding these rules and making safe, legal decisions during every shift.

paragraphs: Permits must be displayed or available at the business location. Inspectors and law enforcement may check licenses at any time. Some businesses must also post visible signs related to underage alcohol laws. You should know where permits are kept and confirm your workplace is operating legally. If a business is found operating without proper licensing, it can face serious consequences, including fines or shutdown. References: SC Code § 61-4-70; § 61-6-1530; § 61-2-90; § 61-2-100; § 61-2-140; Reg. 7-200.1; Reg. 7-202; Reg. 7-200.3.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol laws also control hours of sale. These hours can vary depending on the type of alcohol and location. Some areas may have additional local restrictions. You must stop service when required by law. Continuing to serve outside legal hours can result in penalties. Always follow your employer’s cutoff times and closing procedures.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Only approved businesses can sell certain types of alcohol. For example, liquor sales are more restricted than beer and wine. Some locations may not be allowed to sell specific products at all. Understanding what your establishment is licensed to sell helps prevent illegal service. Never assume all alcohol types are treated the same.

These steps help you avoid violations and protect your job. Safe service starts with understanding the limits placed on your business and your role within it.

paragraphs: Servers can be held responsible for illegal alcohol service. This includes selling to underage individuals or serving outside permitted hours. Even if a manager is present, you are still accountable for your actions. Certain roles also have minimum age rules for employees and bartenders. Knowing the law helps you make correct decisions and avoid personal risk. Always ask questions if you are unsure. References: SC Code § 61-4-90(D); § 61-6-2200; § 61-6-4070; § 61-6-4140.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement and regulatory agencies have the authority to inspect alcohol-serving businesses. These inspections can happen during normal operations. Officers may check identification practices, observe service, and review permits. Cooperation during inspections is required. Interfering with an inspection can lead to additional penalties.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Local governments may set additional rules for alcohol sales. These can include zoning restrictions or local ordinances. Your employer should train you on any location-specific rules. However, it is your responsibility to follow them during service. Always be aware that rules may differ depending on where you work.

interactive.question: You are working at a restaurant. A customer asks if they can purchase a type of alcohol your location does not normally sell. You are unsure if your business is licensed for it. What should you do?

options.label: A. Sell the alcohol if the customer insists
B. Check with a manager before serving
C. Guess based on past experience
D. Tell the customer it is always allowed

feedback.correct: You must confirm what your establishment is licensed to sell. When unsure, always verify before serving. Guessing or assuming can lead to illegal service and potential penalties.

paragraphs: Quick rule: subtract 21 years from today's date; if the customer's birthdate is on or before that date, they are legal. If it's after, refuse the sale.

paragraphs: Practical rule: verify ID consistently for anyone who might be underage; if no valid ID, refuse the sale. References: SC Code § 61-4-50; § 61-6-4080; Reg. 7-200.4.

interactive.question: A young-looking customer orders a drink. They say they forgot their ID but insist they are 21. Their friend offers to confirm their age. What should you do?

feedback.correct: You must verify age with valid identification. Verbal confirmation from others is not acceptable. Serving without ID risks an underage sale and legal consequences.

paragraphs: South Carolina law prohibits serving alcohol to anyone who is intoxicated. This applies to all alcohol types and all licensed establishments. As a server, you must recognize when a customer has had too much to drink. Continuing to serve an intoxicated person increases safety risks and can lead to legal consequences for both you and your employer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Intoxication affects judgment, coordination, and reaction time. Customers may not always realize how impaired they are. It is your responsibility to observe behavior and make decisions based on what you see. Do not rely on what a customer says about their condition. Focus on clear signs and act early to prevent further intoxication.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Common signs of intoxication include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These signs may appear gradually. Watching for changes over time helps you identify when a guest is becoming impaired. Recognizing these indicators early allows you to slow or stop service before the situation worsens.

paragraphs: Once a customer shows clear signs of intoxication, you must stop serving alcohol. This decision should be based on observed behavior, not personal opinion. Waiting too long increases risk. Acting at the right time helps prevent accidents, injuries, and legal issues tied to over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Refusing service can be difficult, but it is required when a customer is intoxicated. Stay calm and respectful. Use simple, clear language when explaining your decision. Do not argue or place blame. If needed, involve a manager for support. Handling the situation professionally helps reduce conflict and keeps you compliant.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina DUI laws make it illegal to operate a vehicle while impaired by alcohol. Impairment can occur before a person feels “drunk.” As a server, your actions can help prevent impaired driving. Stopping service at the right time reduces the chance a customer will leave and drive while impaired. References: SC Code § 56-5-2930; § 56-5-2933; § 56-5-2940; § 56-5-2950; § 56-5-2951; § 61-4-110; § 61-6-4020.

Your goal is not just compliance, but also protecting the customer and the public from alcohol-related harm.

paragraphs: Serving an intoxicated person can lead to legal consequences. Both the server and the business may be held responsible. These consequences may include fines, license actions, or other penalties. Even one mistake can have serious impact. This is why consistent monitoring and decision-making are essential during every shift. References: SC Code § 61-4-580; § 61-6-1830; § 61-6-2220; § 61-6-2230.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Customers may try to continue drinking after you refuse service. Stay firm in your decision. Do not serve additional alcohol once intoxication is clear. If the situation escalates, follow workplace procedures and involve management. Your safety and the safety of others should always come first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Helping intoxicated customers leave safely is part of responsible service. Encourage safe options such as rides from friends, taxis, or rideshare services. Do not allow an intoxicated person to drive if you can intervene safely. Taking action at this stage helps prevent DUI incidents and protects lives.

paragraphs: South Carolina law addresses the presence of concealed weapons in alcohol-serving establishments. Certain locations that sell alcohol for on-premises consumption may restrict firearms. These rules are designed to reduce risk in environments where alcohol is consumed. As a server, you are not expected to enforce weapon laws directly, but you should understand basic restrictions and follow your workplace policies. References: SC Code § 16-23-465; § 23-31-220.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Businesses have the right to set rules about weapons on their property. Some establishments may prohibit firearms entirely. These rules are often posted at entrances. If a location restricts weapons, customers must follow those rules. As a staff member, you should be aware of your workplace policy and report concerns to management.

Safety is the priority. Following proper reporting procedures helps reduce risk and ensures the situation is handled appropriately.

You must cooperate with officers during any interaction. Providing accurate information and following instructions supports compliance and avoids additional issues.

paragraphs: Alcohol service in South Carolina carries legal responsibility. If laws are violated, consequences may apply to both the server and the business. These consequences can fall into three main categories: criminal, civil, and administrative. Understanding these categories helps you recognize the risks tied to your decisions and reinforces why following the law matters every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Criminal liability involves breaking the law. This can include selling alcohol to a minor or serving someone who is clearly intoxicated. If charged, a server may face fines or other legal penalties. These consequences are handled through the criminal justice system. Avoiding illegal service decisions helps protect you from criminal charges.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Criminal violations may include {{item_1}}, serving {{item_2}}, ignoring {{item_3}}, making {{item_4}}, or failing {{item_5}}. These actions break South Carolina law and can result in penalties. Staying alert and following procedures helps prevent these violations from occurring.

paragraphs: Civil liability involves lawsuits. If alcohol service leads to harm, such as an accident or injury, a business or server may be sued. These cases often involve financial damages. Civil cases are separate from criminal charges. Even if no criminal charge is filed, a civil lawsuit can still occur. Some businesses must also maintain liquor liability insurance. References: SC Code § 15-38-15; § 15-38-20; § 15-38-30; § 15-38-40; § 61-2-147; § 61-2-145.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Administrative liability relates to licensing and regulatory action. State agencies can take action against a business permit. This may include fines, suspension, or revocation of the license. These actions directly affect the business’s ability to operate. Violations by staff can lead to consequences for the entire establishment. References: SC Code § 61-4-580; § 61-6-1830; § 61-6-2220; § 61-6-2230.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: All three types of liability can happen from one incident. For example, an underage sale could lead to criminal charges, a lawsuit, and license penalties. This is why every decision matters. Following proper procedures reduces risk and protects both you and your employer from multiple forms of liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Consequences may include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These outcomes can affect your income, career, and future opportunities. Understanding the impact helps reinforce why responsible alcohol service is critical in every situation.

paragraphs: Servers are expected to follow both state law and workplace policies. Policies are designed to help prevent violations. Ignoring these rules increases risk. If you are unsure about a situation, ask a manager before acting. Taking a moment to verify can prevent serious consequences.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Your actions also affect others. Illegal alcohol service can lead to injuries, accidents, or long-term harm. These outcomes may impact customers, families, and the community. Responsible service is not just about avoiding penalties. It is about protecting people from preventable harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers and coworkers are resources when handling difficult situations. If a situation feels uncertain or risky, involve management early. Do not try to handle complex issues alone. Team support helps ensure correct decisions and reduces the chance of mistakes that could lead to liability.

sections.title: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC

bullet_points: 2A. Effects of Alcohol on the Body + Absorption Basics.
text.body: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

text.instruction: Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Alcohol is a {{item_1}} that slows the central nervous system. It affects the brain and body at the same time. As a server, you must understand how alcohol changes behavior. This helps you make safe service decisions. Effects can appear quickly or slowly depending on the person. Always watch for changes, not just drink count.

paragraphs: After a person drinks, alcohol enters the bloodstream. It does not need to be digested like food. Some alcohol is absorbed in the stomach. Most absorption happens in the small intestine. Once in the blood, it travels to the brain. This is why effects can begin within minutes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Alcohol moves through the body in a predictable path. First, it is {{item_1}}. Then it enters the {{item_2}}. Next, it passes into the {{item_3}}. From there, it enters the {{item_4}}. Finally, it reaches the {{item_5}}, where impairment begins. Knowing this path helps explain why drinking faster increases risk.

paragraphs: The liver processes alcohol over time. It breaks down alcohol at a steady rate. Most people process about one standard drink per hour. Drinking faster than this causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. This leads to higher intoxication levels. You cannot speed up this process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) measures the amount of alcohol in the blood. As BAC rises, impairment increases. Even small amounts can affect judgment and coordination. Higher BAC levels lead to more serious impairment. Servers should focus on visible signs, not exact BAC numbers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Alcohol affects the brain in stages. Early signs include {{item_1}} and {{item_2}}. As drinking continues, people may show {{item_3}} and {{item_4}}. At higher levels, you may see {{item_5}} and {{item_6}}. These signs help you decide when to slow or stop service.

paragraphs: Not everyone reacts to alcohol the same way. Body size, gender, and health all play a role. Food in the stomach can slow absorption. Medications may increase effects. Experience with alcohol may change how signs appear. Always judge each guest individually.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Several factors affect intoxication speed. Drinking on an {{item_1}} increases absorption. Larger drinks raise BAC {{item_2}}. Mixing alcohol with {{item_3}} can increase impairment. Drinking multiple beverages in {{item_4}} raises risk. These factors help explain why some guests become impaired faster.

paragraphs: Alcohol impacts coordination and decision-making. Guests may take risks they normally avoid. Reaction time slows. This increases the chance of injury or unsafe behavior. As a server, you must recognize these changes early. Acting sooner helps prevent problems.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, behavior may change. Some guests become louder or more emotional. Others may become quiet or withdrawn. Physical signs often appear with behavior changes. Watch for patterns, not just one sign. Consistent observation is key to safe service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Common physical signs include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, and {{item_3}}. You may notice {{item_4}} or {{item_5}}. These signs often appear together. Recognizing multiple indicators helps confirm intoxication. Use these observations to guide your service decisions.

interactive.question: A guest orders several drinks quickly. Within a short time, you notice slower speech and unsteady movement. The guest insists they feel fine and asks for another drink. Based on alcohol absorption and effects, what is the safest action?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Serve one more drink slowly
B. Ignore behavior and continue service
C. Offer food and continue alcohol service
D. Refuse further alcohol service

feedback.correct: Refusing service is the safest choice when clear signs of impairment appear. Alcohol is already affecting the guest, and continued service increases risk. Servers must act based on observed behavior, not guest statements.

paragraphs: Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) measures how much alcohol is in a person’s bloodstream. It is shown as a percentage. For example, 0.08% means alcohol is present in the blood at that level. As BAC increases, impairment increases. Servers do not measure BAC directly, but understanding it helps explain behavior and risk.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: BAC rises when alcohol enters the bloodstream faster than the body can process it. The liver removes alcohol at a steady rate. Drinking multiple beverages quickly causes BAC to increase. This leads to stronger effects and higher impairment. Timing and pace matter as much as total drinks consumed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: BAC is affected by several key factors. Body {{item_1}} impacts how alcohol is distributed. {{item_2}} can influence processing speed. Drinking on an {{item_3}} increases absorption. {{item_4}} affects how much alcohol is consumed. Drinking {{item_5}} raises BAC faster. These factors explain why guests respond differently.

paragraphs: A standard drink is a way to measure alcohol content. Different beverages can contain similar amounts of alcohol. Beer, wine, and liquor vary in size but may have equal alcohol levels. Understanding standard drinks helps you track how much alcohol a guest consumes, even with different beverage types.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Not all drinks are equal in strength. A large pour or strong mix can contain more alcohol than one standard drink. Guests may not realize how much they are consuming. As a server, you should recognize when drinks are stronger than usual. This helps prevent over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Common standard drink examples include a {{item_1}}, a {{item_2}}, or a {{item_3}} pour. Each contains about the same amount of alcohol. Larger servings or higher alcohol content increase total intake. Always consider both {{item_4}} and {{item_5}} when evaluating consumption.

paragraphs: BAC does not decrease quickly. Time is the only factor that lowers BAC. Coffee, food, or cold showers do not speed up the process. These may make a person feel more alert, but impairment remains. Never rely on myths when making service decisions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As BAC rises, judgment and coordination decline. Guests may take risks or ignore consequences. This increases the chance of harm. Servers must focus on behavior, not just drink count. Recognizing patterns early allows you to slow or stop service before problems occur.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Higher BAC levels lead to more serious effects. Guests may show {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, or {{item_3}}. At higher levels, {{item_4}} and {{item_5}} may occur. These signs indicate increased risk. Recognizing these changes supports safe and responsible service decisions.

paragraphs: Tracking drinks helps you estimate risk, but it is not exact. Two guests may drink the same amount and show different effects. Always combine drink tracking with observation. This approach improves accuracy and supports compliance with South Carolina service responsibilities.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Servers play a key role in preventing over-service. Understanding BAC and standard drinks helps guide decisions. You are responsible for noticing changes and taking action. Slowing service, offering alternatives, or stopping alcohol service may be necessary to protect the guest and others.

interactive.question: A guest orders two strong mixed drinks in a short time. You notice faster speech and reduced coordination. The guest asks for another drink right away. Based on BAC and standard drink understanding, what is the safest action?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Serve another drink with extra ice
B. Offer water and slow service
C. Ignore signs and continue service
D. Suggest switching to beer and continue

feedback.correct: Slowing service and offering water helps reduce risk. Strong drinks increase BAC quickly, especially in a short time. Recognizing early signs and adjusting service supports responsible alcohol service and aligns with your role.

paragraphs: BAC is affected by more than just how much a person drinks. Individual factors change how alcohol is absorbed and processed. As a server, you must understand these differences. This helps explain why two guests may show different levels of impairment after similar drinking. Always focus on behavior and risk, not assumptions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Body weight plays a major role in BAC. People with lower body weight usually reach higher BAC levels faster. There is less body mass to absorb the alcohol. Heavier individuals may show effects more slowly, but they can still become impaired. Never assume someone is safe based on size alone.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Several personal factors affect BAC. A person’s {{item_1}} changes alcohol concentration. {{item_2}} can influence how alcohol is processed. {{item_3}} affects distribution in the body. {{item_4}} conditions may slow processing. These differences explain why alcohol impacts each guest differently.

paragraphs: Food in the stomach slows alcohol absorption. Drinking on an empty stomach allows alcohol to enter the bloodstream faster. This can lead to quicker intoxication. Guests who have not eaten are at higher risk. As a server, you should be aware of how eating patterns affect alcohol impact.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: The rate of drinking is one of the biggest factors affecting BAC. Drinking multiple beverages quickly causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. Slower drinking gives the body time to process alcohol. Fast consumption increases the risk of impairment and unsafe behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Drinking patterns strongly affect BAC. Consuming drinks in {{item_1}} raises levels quickly. Having multiple drinks {{item_2}} increases risk. Stronger drinks raise BAC {{item_3}}. Mixing alcohol with {{item_4}} may mask effects. These patterns help you identify high-risk situations early.

paragraphs: Medications and drugs can increase alcohol’s effects. Prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal drugs may interact with alcohol. These interactions can cause stronger impairment or unexpected behavior. As a server, you may not know what a guest has taken. Focus on visible signs and changes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Tolerance refers to how a person responds to alcohol over time. Some people appear less affected after drinking. This does not mean alcohol is not impacting them. Their BAC may still be high. Tolerance can hide visible signs, making careful observation even more important.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Tolerance can be misleading. A guest may seem {{item_1}} but still be impaired. They may show fewer {{item_2}} despite high BAC. Reaction time can still be {{item_3}}. Judgment may still be {{item_4}}. Never rely on tolerance to decide whether to continue service.

paragraphs: Environmental factors can also influence alcohol effects. Heat, fatigue, and stress may increase impairment. Loud or crowded settings may make it harder to notice signs. Stay alert and focused on each guest. Your awareness helps prevent over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Understanding BAC factors helps you make better service decisions. You cannot measure BAC directly, but you can recognize risk. Combine drink tracking with observation. When in doubt, slow service or stop serving alcohol. Protecting the guest and others is your responsibility.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

interactive.question: A guest has been drinking slowly but mentions they have not eaten all day. After two drinks, you notice quicker mood changes and reduced coordination. The guest asks for another drink. What is the safest action?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Serve another drink since intake is low
B. Offer food and continue normal service
C. Slow or stop alcohol service based on signs
D. Ignore food factor and continue service

feedback.correct: Drinking on an empty stomach increases BAC more quickly. Even with fewer drinks, impairment can occur sooner. Observing changes and adjusting service helps prevent over-service and supports responsible alcohol service.

paragraphs: Alcohol elimination is the process of removing alcohol from the body. This happens mainly in the liver. The liver breaks down alcohol at a steady rate. Once alcohol is in the bloodstream, the body must process it over time. This process cannot be rushed or skipped.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: On average, the body eliminates about one standard drink per hour. This rate can vary slightly by person. However, it does not speed up significantly. Drinking faster than this rate causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. This leads to increasing impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Alcohol leaves the body through a slow process. Most is broken down by the {{item_1}}. Small amounts leave through {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, and {{item_4}}. These minor paths do not remove alcohol quickly. The liver does most of the work. Time is the only reliable way to reduce BAC.

paragraphs: Many people believe certain actions can reduce intoxication quickly. These are common myths. While some actions may make a person feel more alert, they do not lower BAC. Servers must understand the difference between feeling better and being less impaired.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Coffee is a common example. It may increase alertness, but it does not remove alcohol from the body. A guest may seem more awake but still be impaired. This can increase risk because they may believe they are safe when they are not.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Common myths about sobering up include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, and {{item_3}}. These may change how a person feels, but they do not reduce BAC. Only {{item_4}} lowers alcohol levels. Believing these myths can lead to unsafe decisions and increased risk.

paragraphs: Food can slow alcohol absorption if eaten before or during drinking. However, once alcohol is in the bloodstream, food does not remove it. Guests may think eating after drinking will sober them up. This is not accurate.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Fresh air may help a person feel better, but it does not reduce BAC. A guest may appear more stable after stepping outside. However, impairment remains. Always rely on observed signs, not temporary improvements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: False beliefs can lead to unsafe behavior. A guest may feel {{item_1}} after coffee but still be impaired. They may think they can {{item_2}}, which is dangerous. Visible signs like {{item_3}} and {{item_4}} still matter. Always trust what you observe.

paragraphs: As a server, you must act based on facts, not myths. If a guest shows signs of intoxication, you should slow or stop alcohol service. Do not rely on claims that they have “sobered up.” Your responsibility is to prevent harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Understanding elimination helps you make safer decisions. Alcohol takes time to leave the body. Guests cannot speed up this process. Your actions can reduce risk by limiting further alcohol service and offering safer alternatives.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

interactive.question: A guest drinks several beverages and then orders coffee. They say they are fine to continue drinking because the coffee “sobers them up.” What is the safest response?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Serve another drink since they had coffee
B. Allow time and continue normal service
C. Offer food and continue alcohol service
D. Stop alcohol service based on impairment

feedback.correct: Coffee does not reduce BAC. The guest may feel more alert but is still impaired. Stopping alcohol service is the safest choice when signs of intoxication are present.

paragraphs: Alcohol can interact with many types of drugs. These include prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal substances. When combined, effects can become stronger or unpredictable. As a server, you may not know what a guest has taken. Focus on recognizing unusual or increased impairment and respond appropriately.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Drug interactions can increase intoxication quickly. A person may appear impaired after only one or two drinks. This can happen even if they normally handle alcohol well. These situations require careful observation and early action to prevent harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Alcohol can interact with different drug types. {{item_1}} may increase drowsiness. {{item_2}} can slow reactions. {{item_3}} may create unpredictable effects. Combining substances can increase {{item_4}} and {{item_5}}. These interactions are a key safety concern for servers.

paragraphs: Some prescription medications warn against alcohol use. These include drugs for pain, anxiety, sleep, and mood. Mixing alcohol with these medications can cause stronger sedation or confusion. Guests may not always follow warnings, so you must rely on what you observe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Over-the-counter medications can also affect alcohol response. Cold medicines and antihistamines may increase drowsiness. Even common medications can change how alcohol affects the body. Do not assume OTC use is harmless when combined with alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Common OTC risks include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, and {{item_3}}. These effects may increase when combined with alcohol. Guests may not realize the impact. Watch for {{item_4}} and {{item_5}} in behavior when serving alcohol.

paragraphs: Illegal drugs can create dangerous combinations with alcohol. Effects may include confusion, aggression, or extreme drowsiness. These reactions can appear suddenly. As a server, you must respond quickly if a guest shows unusual or severe impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Mixing alcohol with stimulants can be especially risky. A guest may feel alert but still be impaired. This can lead to risky decisions. The masking effect makes it harder to judge intoxication. Focus on behavior and coordination, not energy level.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Some combinations hide true impairment. A guest may seem {{item_1}} but still have {{item_2}}. They may show {{item_3}} but {{item_4}}. These mixed signals increase risk. Always rely on multiple signs before continuing service decisions.

paragraphs: Drug and alcohol interactions can increase the chance of injury or illness. Guests may lose coordination or awareness. This creates safety risks for them and others. Early intervention helps prevent serious outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you suspect drug interaction, slow or stop alcohol service. Offer water or non-alcoholic options. Involve a manager if needed. Stay calm and professional. Your goal is to reduce risk and keep the situation safe.

feedback.correct: Drug interactions can increase alcohol effects quickly. Even one drink may cause strong impairment. Stopping alcohol service is the safest choice when signs appear.

sections.title: Unit 3 — South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability

bullet_points: 3A. Importance of Preventing Underage Sales + Life Impact.
3C. ID Anyone Who Appears Underage (Always Rule).
3D. Valid Forms of ID + Legal Requirements.
3H. Detecting Fake IDs (Security Features & Red Flags).
text.body: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Preventing underage alcohol sales is a core responsibility in South Carolina. Selling to a minor is illegal and can lead to serious consequences for both the server and the business. Your actions directly impact safety, compliance, and your job security. Every sale decision matters. Following the law protects you, your workplace, and your community from avoidable harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Minors face higher risks when alcohol is involved. Their bodies and decision-making skills are still developing. Alcohol use at a young age increases the chance of unsafe behavior and injury. Preventing access reduces these risks. As a server, you play a key role in limiting harm by refusing sales when required and following proper procedures every time.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Preventing underage sales helps reduce {{item_1}} for you and your employer. It also limits {{item_2}} that can affect operations. Your actions help prevent {{item_3}} such as accidents or violence. Most importantly, you reduce {{item_4}} by stopping unsafe access to alcohol before it leads to serious consequences.

paragraphs: South Carolina law requires that alcohol not be sold or served to anyone under 21. Violations can result in penalties for both the server and the establishment. This may include fines or other consequences. Following the law is required. It protects your job and ensures your workplace stays compliant with state requirements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Checking identification is one of the most effective ways to prevent underage sales. You must verify age when there is any doubt. Never rely on appearance alone. Consistent ID checks reduce mistakes. Even one failure can result in a violation. Make ID verification a routine part of your service process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Underage drinking can harm education and future opportunities; some offenses may affect scholarship eligibility. Refusing a sale protects their future. References: SC Code § 59-104-20; § 61-4-100; § 61-6-4085; Reg. 62-900.95.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Your responsibility includes making decisions that follow the law and company policies. Refusing a sale may feel uncomfortable, but it is necessary. Staying calm and confident helps you handle these situations correctly. Doing the right thing protects you, your employer, and others in the environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: When refusing a sale, stay {{item_1}} and use a {{item_2}}. Clearly state {{item_3}} without arguing. Follow {{item_4}} and involve {{item_5}} if needed. These steps help you enforce the law safely while maintaining control of the situation.

paragraphs: Minors may attempt to buy alcohol using fake or borrowed identification. Stay alert and follow proper ID-checking steps. If something does not seem right, take time to verify. Do not rush. Careful checking helps prevent illegal sales and protects you from making a costly mistake.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers are a resource when situations become difficult. If a customer becomes pushy or confrontational, ask for help. You are not expected to handle every situation alone. Team support helps ensure rules are followed consistently and safely across the business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency is key to preventing underage sales. Apply the same process to every customer. Do not make exceptions based on pressure or assumptions. Following the same steps each time reduces risk. Consistent actions help protect you and ensure compliance with South Carolina alcohol laws.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

interactive.question: A customer appears young but insists they are 21. You are unsure and the line is growing. What should you do?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Complete the sale quickly
B. Ask another customer
C. Skip the ID check
D. Request valid ID before selling

feedback.correct: Always verify age when unsure. Requesting valid ID is required to prevent underage sales and stay compliant with South Carolina law.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, alcohol servers are legally responsible for following state laws when selling or serving alcohol. This includes refusing sales to minors and not serving intoxicated individuals. You are accountable for your actions during every shift. Failing to follow the law can result in penalties for you and your employer. Understanding your responsibility helps you make better decisions in real situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Selling alcohol to a person under 21 is illegal in South Carolina. This applies even if the customer appears older or presents questionable identification. You are expected to verify age before completing a sale. If you fail to do so, you may face legal consequences. Following proper ID procedures helps you avoid violations and stay compliant with the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Servers can face {{item_1}} and possible {{item_2}} for illegal sales. Your employer may face {{item_3}} that impact the business. Violations can also lead to {{item_4}} and damage your work history. These consequences are serious and avoidable when you follow proper procedures.

paragraphs: Serving alcohol to an intoxicated person is also prohibited. You must recognize signs of impairment and stop service when necessary. Continuing to serve someone who is clearly intoxicated increases risk. It can lead to harm, including accidents or injuries. Your role includes monitoring customers and making decisions that protect safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Legal responsibility applies to both individual servers and the business. Even if a manager is present, you are still accountable for your actions. You cannot ignore the law because of pressure or instructions from others. Each server must follow legal requirements during every transaction. Personal accountability is part of responsible alcohol service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Criminal penalties may apply if laws are broken. These penalties depend on the situation and the violation. Selling to a minor or serving an intoxicated person can result in charges. Legal consequences can affect your record and future employment. Following the law helps you avoid these outcomes and protects your professional reputation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Administrative penalties may impact the business. This can include fines or actions taken against the establishment’s license. These penalties can affect all employees. Even one violation can cause problems for the entire operation. Your actions contribute to the overall compliance of the business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Types of liability include {{item_1}} for breaking laws, {{item_2}} if harm occurs, and {{item_3}} actions against the business. Understanding these categories helps you see how one decision can affect multiple areas. Staying compliant reduces risk across all levels of responsibility.

paragraphs: Civil liability may occur if someone is harmed after being served alcohol illegally. This could involve injuries or damages linked to alcohol service. While you may not see immediate effects, your actions can contribute to serious outcomes. Preventing illegal service helps reduce the risk of harm and legal claims.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement and regulatory agencies monitor alcohol sales. This may include compliance checks or investigations. You are expected to follow the law at all times, not just when being observed. Consistent compliance helps you avoid violations and ensures your workplace meets state requirements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Making the correct decision in the moment is critical. If you are unsure about a situation, stop and verify. Ask for identification or involve a manager. Do not guess or take shortcuts. Taking a few extra seconds to follow proper steps can prevent serious legal consequences.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

B. Accept their verbal confirmation

feedback.correct: You must verify age with valid identification. If a customer cannot provide it, you are required to refuse the sale to remain compliant with South Carolina law.

paragraphs: Practical rule: verify ID consistently for anyone who might be underage; if no valid ID, refuse the sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Brief definition: 'appears underage' means anyone who could reasonably be under 21. When in doubt, verify ID.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: The Always Rule means you must {{item_1}} when someone {{item_2}}, before {{item_3}}, without {{item_4}}. This keeps your decisions consistent and helps prevent mistakes that could lead to violations.

paragraphs: Failing to check ID is one of the most common reasons for underage sales. Even if a customer is actually 21, you can still face consequences if you did not verify their age. The law requires proper ID checking, not assumptions. Taking the extra step protects you and your workplace.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency: use the same ID-checking process every time; avoid exceptions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Busy environments can create pressure to move quickly. Long lines or impatient customers may tempt you to skip steps. Do not let this affect your decisions. Taking a few seconds to check ID is always worth it. Speed should never replace proper procedure when alcohol is involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Checking ID also protects minors from harm. Preventing access to alcohol reduces the risk of unsafe behavior and poor decisions. Your role is not just about following rules. You are helping create a safer environment by stopping illegal sales before they happen.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: When checking ID, focus on {{item_1}}, confirm {{item_2}}, look for {{item_3}}, and take your {{item_4}}. These steps help ensure you are making a correct and lawful decision before completing the sale.

paragraphs: If a customer refuses to show ID, you must refuse the sale. There are no exceptions to this rule. A valid ID is required to verify age. Do not accept verbal confirmation or pressure from the customer. Following this rule protects you from making an illegal sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers and coworkers can support you when enforcing ID rules. If a situation becomes difficult, ask for help. You are not expected to handle confrontational situations alone. Team support helps ensure rules are followed consistently and safely across the workplace.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Building the habit of always checking ID makes your job easier. When it becomes routine, you are less likely to make mistakes. Strong habits reduce risk and improve consistency. Over time, this approach helps protect you, your employer, and your customers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

interactive.question: A customer looks about 25 and says they forgot their ID. They insist they are old enough. What should you do?
interactive.instruction: Read the scenario, then select the best answer.

options.label: A. Complete the sale based on appearance
B. Accept their verbal confirmation
C. Ask a coworker to decide
D. Refuse the sale without ID

feedback.correct: You must verify age with valid identification. If a customer cannot provide ID, you are required to refuse the sale to stay compliant with South Carolina

paragraphs: In South Carolina, you must verify a customer’s age using valid identification before selling alcohol when required. Not all IDs are acceptable. You are responsible for knowing which forms are valid. Accepting the wrong ID can lead to an illegal sale. Always take time to review identification carefully before completing a transaction.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Valid identification must be government-issued and include key details. This includes a photo, date of birth, and identifying information. The ID must allow you to confirm the customer is at least 21 years old. If any required element is missing or unclear, you should not accept the ID.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: A valid ID must include a {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, and {{item_4}}. These features help confirm identity and age. If any of these elements are missing or appear altered, the ID should not be accepted.

paragraphs: Common valid forms of identification include a driver’s license issued by a U.S. state. These IDs are widely used and contain the required information. You should still examine them closely. Do not assume an ID is valid just because it looks familiar. Always verify the details before completing a sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: State-issued identification cards are also acceptable. These are issued to individuals who do not drive but still need official identification. They include similar features to a driver’s license. As with all IDs, check the photo, date of birth, and expiration date before accepting it.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Military identification may also be presented. These IDs are issued by the U.S. government and include identifying information. Review the ID carefully to confirm it belongs to the person presenting it. If you are unsure how to verify it, ask a manager for assistance before proceeding.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Expired identification is not valid for alcohol sales. Even if the date of birth shows the customer is over 21, an expired ID should not be accepted. Expiration dates must be current. Always check this detail as part of your standard ID verification process.

bullet_points: 4A. What Intoxication Is + Stages of Impairment.
4F. Recognizing Problem Drinking + When to Act.
text.body: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Intoxication means a person has consumed enough alcohol to impair normal mental or physical abilities. This affects judgment, coordination, and reaction time. In South Carolina, you must not serve someone who is overly intoxicated. Your responsibility is to recognize impairment early and act before it creates risk for the guest, your team, or the public.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol is a depressant that slows the central nervous system. As drinking increases, brain function becomes less controlled. Decision-making declines, and reaction time slows. Physical coordination also weakens. Even small increases in alcohol can change behavior. Understanding these effects helps you identify intoxication before it becomes obvious or dangerous.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Intoxication develops in stages. Early signs may seem minor but can escalate quickly. Watch for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These early changes often appear before severe impairment. Spotting them early allows you to adjust service and prevent illegal or unsafe outcomes.

paragraphs: The early stage of impairment often includes mood changes and reduced inhibitions. Guests may become more talkative or confident. They may drink faster or encourage others to drink. While this may seem harmless, it signals the start of impaired judgment. This is the best time to slow service and monitor behavior closely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, coordination and focus begin to decline. Guests may struggle to follow conversations or track time. They may forget what they ordered or repeat themselves. These signs show alcohol is affecting brain function more significantly. At this point, continued service increases risk and may lead to illegal over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Mid-stage impairment includes more visible physical and mental changes. Watch for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These are clear warning signs that the guest is becoming intoxicated. Service should be slowed or stopped to prevent further impairment and legal violations.

paragraphs: In later stages, intoxication becomes obvious and serious. Guests may stumble, drop items, or have trouble standing. Speech may be difficult to understand. Emotional changes like aggression or sadness may appear. At this point, the guest should not be served alcohol under any circumstances. Immediate action is required to manage the situation safely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Severe intoxication can lead to dangerous health risks. A guest may become unresponsive or unable to stay awake. Breathing may slow, and vomiting may occur. These are medical warning signs. Alcohol poisoning is possible. This situation requires immediate attention, including involving management and seeking medical help if needed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Behavioral changes can signal increasing intoxication. Look for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These behaviors can create safety risks for staff and other guests. Addressing them early helps prevent escalation and supports a safer environment.

paragraphs: Not all guests show intoxication the same way. Some may appear calm while still being impaired. Others may become loud or emotional. You must rely on observable signs, not assumptions. Consistent monitoring is key. Always base your decisions on behavior, not how much a guest claims they have consumed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Your responsibility is to recognize and respond appropriately at each stage. Take action by {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These steps help prevent over-service and keep you compliant with South Carolina law and workplace policies.

feedback.correct: These signs show clear physical and mental impairment. Mid-stage intoxication includes slurred speech, poor coordination, and confusion. At this point, you should stop or significantly limit alcohol service to prevent further intoxication and remain compliant with the law.

paragraphs: Physical signs of intoxication are visible changes in how a person moves, speaks, or appears. These signs help you identify impairment without relying on what a guest says. In South Carolina, recognizing these signs is critical because you must not serve someone who is overly intoxicated. Your decision must be based on what you observe, not assumptions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As alcohol affects the body, coordination begins to decline. Guests may have trouble with simple movements like picking up a drink or standing up. Small physical mistakes are often early indicators. Paying attention to these changes allows you to adjust service before impairment becomes severe or unsafe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Watch for early physical warning signs such as {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These signs may appear subtle at first. They indicate alcohol is beginning to affect motor skills. Early recognition helps you slow service and prevent further intoxication.

paragraphs: Balance and coordination continue to worsen as intoxication increases. A guest may bump into objects or need support while walking. They may misjudge distances or struggle to sit down properly. These physical signs show the body is losing control, which increases the risk of injury and unsafe behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Hand-eye coordination is often affected during intoxication. Guests may spill drinks, miss objects, or fumble with money or cards. These mistakes may seem minor but are important indicators. They show that alcohol is impairing basic motor skills and that continued service could make the situation worse.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Mid-level intoxication often includes stronger physical symptoms. Look for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These are clear signs that the guest is impaired. At this point, you should strongly consider stopping alcohol service to stay compliant and safe.

paragraphs: Speech patterns can reveal intoxication. Words may become slurred, sentences may trail off, or the guest may repeat themselves. Speech changes are a reliable sign that alcohol is affecting brain function. These signs should not be ignored when deciding whether to continue service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Facial appearance can also change with intoxication. A guest may have flushed skin, drooping eyelids, or a blank expression. Eyes may appear unfocused or watery. These physical changes provide additional evidence of impairment and should be considered along with other observed behaviors.

paragraphs: Behavioral signs of intoxication are changes in how a person acts, speaks, or interacts with others. These signs often appear before severe physical symptoms. In South Carolina, recognizing these behaviors is critical because you must not serve alcohol to an overly intoxicated person. Your decisions should be based on observable behavior, not assumptions or what the guest says.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, behavior becomes less controlled. Guests may have trouble focusing on conversations or following directions. They may forget what they ordered or repeat themselves. These signs indicate that alcohol is affecting brain function and decision-making more significantly.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Emotional changes are common with intoxication. A guest may become unusually happy, angry, or sad without a clear reason. Mood swings can happen quickly. These changes can lead to unpredictable behavior, which increases the risk of conflict or unsafe situations in your establishment.

paragraphs: Environmental and individual risk factors affect how quickly a person becomes intoxicated. These factors can increase or decrease impairment, even if two people drink the same amount. content_structure: Key individual factors include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These factors influence how alcohol affects the body. Understanding them helps you anticipate how quickly a guest may become impaired and adjust your service decisions accordingly.

paragraphs: Food intake plays a major role in alcohol absorption. Guests who drink on an empty stomach may become intoxicated faster. Food slows the absorption of alcohol into the bloodstream. If a guest has not eaten, you should be more cautious and monitor for early signs of impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Medications and drugs can increase the effects of alcohol. Prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal substances may interact with alcohol in unpredictable ways. This can lead to faster or stronger impairment. You may not always know what a guest has taken, so watch closely for unusual or rapid changes in behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Drinking patterns also affect intoxication. Watch for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These behaviors increase the risk of rapid intoxication. Slowing service in these situations helps reduce risk and maintain control.

paragraphs: Environmental factors can influence drinking behavior. Loud music, crowded spaces, and group settings may encourage faster drinking. Guests may feel pressured to keep up with others. These conditions can lead to quicker intoxication, even if the guest does not intend to drink heavily.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Time is another important factor. Alcohol takes time to affect the body. A guest may not appear intoxicated immediately after drinking. Effects can build over time. This delay can lead to over-service if you are not careful. Monitor guests consistently, even if they seem fine at first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

content_structure: Environmental risks include {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These conditions often lead to increased drinking. Being aware of these risks helps you stay proactive and prevent over-service before it becomes a problem.

paragraphs: Fatigue and stress can also increase the effects of alcohol. A tired or stressed guest may become impaired more quickly. These factors are not always visible, but they can affect behavior. Pay attention to signs of rapid change, even if the guest has not consumed many drinks.

paragraphs: Preventing over-service means taking action before a guest becomes overly intoxicated. In South Carolina, you must not serve alcohol to someone who is already intoxicated. paragraphs: Serving one drink at a time helps you stay in control. Avoid delivering multiple alcoholic drinks to one guest at once. This reduces the chance of rapid consumption. It also gives you time to observe behavior between drinks and make better decisions about continued service.

bullet_points: 5A. When to Refuse Service + Legal Justification.
text.body: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Refusing service is a legal and professional responsibility in South Carolina. You must not serve alcohol to someone who is underage or already intoxicated. This is not optional. Failing to refuse service can lead to penalties for both you and your employer. Your decisions directly impact safety, compliance, and your job security.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You must refuse service if a customer is under 21. This includes any situation where valid identification is not provided. If you cannot confirm age, you cannot complete the sale. Guessing or assuming age is never acceptable. Always rely on proper ID verification before serving alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Refusal is required when a patron shows signs of intoxication such as {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, or {{item_5}}. These signs indicate impairment. Continuing to serve increases risk. You are expected to recognize these indicators and take action before the situation escalates.

paragraphs: South Carolina law prohibits selling alcohol to an intoxicated person. This applies even if the customer was served elsewhere first. If they are visibly impaired, you must refuse service. It does not matter how they became intoxicated. Your responsibility begins the moment you interact with them.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Legal justification for refusal is based on protecting public safety. Serving an intoxicated person increases the risk of accidents, injuries, and illegal behavior. Refusing service helps prevent harm and keeps your business compliant with state law. It also protects you from personal liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You are also required to refuse service if a customer becomes disruptive or unsafe. This includes threatening behavior, harassment, or refusal to follow rules. Alcohol service should never continue in a situation that risks harm to staff or other customers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: You should involve management when refusing service in situations like {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, or {{item_5}}. Managers can support your decision and help control the situation. Never feel you must handle difficult refusals alone.

paragraphs: Refusal should be firm, calm, and respectful. Do not argue or debate with the customer. Clearly state that you cannot serve them and avoid blaming language. Keep your tone professional and focused on policy and safety, not personal judgment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not offer alternatives that involve alcohol. Instead, suggest non-alcoholic options or food if appropriate. In some cases, offering water or helping arrange transportation may be appropriate. These actions support safety without violating service laws.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Always follow your establishment’s policies when refusing service. This may include documenting the incident or notifying a supervisor. Consistent enforcement helps protect the business and shows compliance with training and legal expectations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Refusal protects you from {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. Making the correct decision may feel uncomfortable, but it prevents serious consequences. Choosing not to serve is always safer than risking a violation.

feedback.correct: Refusing service is required when a customer shows clear signs of intoxication. Involving management helps ensure safety and proper handling of the situation.

paragraphs: Refusing service should follow a clear, consistent process. This helps you stay professional and reduces conflict. A step-by-step approach keeps your actions aligned with the law and your workplace policies. It also shows confidence and control in difficult situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 1: Observe the customer carefully. Look for signs of intoxication or behavior that requires refusal. Do not rush the decision. Take a moment to confirm what you are seeing before acting. Accurate observation is the foundation of a proper refusal.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: When observing, watch for {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These signs help you decide if refusal is necessary. Focus on what the customer is doing right now, not how they appeared earlier.

paragraphs: Step 2: Decide clearly and confidently. Once you determine that service should stop, commit to that decision. Do not hesitate or second-guess yourself. Waiting too long can make the situation worse and increase risk for everyone involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 3: Choose the right moment. Refuse service before pouring or delivering another drink. Timing matters. Acting early prevents escalation and keeps the interaction more manageable. Do not wait until the customer becomes more impaired.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 4: Stay calm and professional. Your tone and body language should remain controlled. Avoid showing frustration or anger. A calm approach reduces tension and makes it easier to communicate your decision clearly.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Use clear language like {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These phrases keep the message simple and focused. Avoid long explanations or emotional statements.

paragraphs: Step 5: Be direct and respectful. Clearly tell the customer you cannot serve them more alcohol. Do not argue or debate. Keep your explanation short and based on policy or safety. Respectful communication helps prevent escalation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 6: Do not negotiate. Once you refuse service, the decision is final. Do not offer “just one more” or make exceptions. Changing your decision weakens your authority and creates confusion for the customer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 7: Stay aware of your surroundings. Watch how the customer reacts. Be ready to involve management or coworkers if needed. Your safety and the safety of others should always come first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: If needed, move to {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These actions help you stay in control of the situation. Always follow your workplace procedures.

paragraphs: This section continues the refusal process after you have told the customer you cannot serve more alcohol. What you do next is just as important as the refusal itself. Proper follow-through helps prevent escalation, protects safety, and ensures compliance with South Carolina expectations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 8: Do not continue alcohol service under any circumstance. Once you refuse, the decision stands. Do not serve the customer again during that visit. Consistency is critical. Changing your decision increases risk and weakens enforcement.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: After refusal, avoid {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These actions can undo your decision and create confusion. Stay consistent and clear in your actions.

paragraphs: Step 9: Offer non-alcoholic alternatives when appropriate. You may suggest water or food. This can help de-escalate the situation and support customer safety. Do not suggest anything that includes alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 10: Monitor the customer’s behavior. Continue observing after refusal. Watch for signs of escalation, aggression, or worsening impairment. Staying aware helps you respond quickly if the situation changes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 11: Involve management when needed. If the customer becomes upset, refuses to comply, or creates a disturbance, notify a manager immediately. Managers are responsible for supporting staff and handling difficult situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Escalate when you see {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, or {{item_5}}. These are warning signs that the situation may require additional support.

paragraphs: Step 12: Keep a safe distance. Do not crowd the customer or invade their space. Position yourself where you can exit if needed. Your safety always comes first. Avoid physical contact unless absolutely necessary and directed by policy.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 13: Follow house procedures for documentation. Some workplaces require incident reports when service is refused. Accurate documentation helps protect the business and shows that proper steps were taken.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 14: Support safe outcomes. If appropriate, help arrange a safe ride or encourage the customer to avoid driving. While not required in every situation, promoting safety aligns with responsible service practices.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

content_structure: Encourage safe choices like {{item_1}}, {{item_2}}, {{item_3}}, {{item_4}}, and {{item_5}}. These actions reduce risk after refusal and support public safety.
